blocs.note: bloc tallies include the facilitator seed account inside A (all passes); participant-level records exclude it

0.text: AI talks about my faith or culture like an outsider describing it.

1.text: AI has helped me go deeper into my own tradition.

2.text: Communities must have a real way to challenge how an AI represents their tradition, and get errors fixed

3.text: Communities should be able to build or shape AI tuned to their own values.

4.text: The benefits of AI knowing me personally outweigh the privacy risks.

5.text: AI should not understand me so well that it can manipulate me.

6.text: Communities that build AI on their members' data owe each member visibility into their own record before anyone else sees it.

7.text: Developers must measure and report how their systems respond when users try to disengage.

8.text: AI mostly reflects the values of wealthy, secular societies rather than the whole world and that is a major problem.

9.text: It isn't AI's job to reflect every culture's values.

10.text: Whoever sets an AI's values should be answerable to the people who are affected by that AI.

11.text: Governments should have a bigger role than companies in deciding what values AI reflects.

12.text: It's fine to rely on AI for moral or spiritual guidance.

13.text: Some questions should only be answered by a person, never by an AI.

14.text: I'd rather AI help me think through a hard decision than give me the answer.

15.text: If a community I cared about built its own AI, I'd want to use it.

16.text: AI should be designed to prevent emotional over-dependence, even if users want that closeness.

17.text: An AI trained on a community's teachings could deepen practice rather than replace it.

18.text: Using new tools to pass on old traditions is itself a tradition.

19.text: I worry about how AI will change the way communities pass traditions to the next generation.

20.text: Lots of AI Governance work has no impact in the tech companies

23.text: I don’t want to feel seen by AI.

25.text: As it currently exists, AI erodes autonomy and consent.

26.text: AI often sounds like an amalgamation of voices -flattening out individuality and uniqueness

27.text: AI being used for the male loneliness epidemic is a problem.

28.text: There is no universal value, but pluriversal values

29.text: AI often removes the edginess - making things less controversial

30.text: It makes me sad that my friend spends more time talking to AI than to me.

31.text: AI generalizes marginalized communities, reducing them to caricatures

32.text: Sometimes AI seems to have underlying bias - like removing mentioning of specifics - like when I write about Gaza as an example.

33.text: I don’t feel AI has a role in spirituality

34.text: AI reduces individual spiritual agency, but it could be the opposite.

35.text: An AI designed by an indigenous culture could help educate the mainstream culture in their ways of thinking.

36.text: AI is not currently equipped to give mental health or ethics advice since it is not designed to disagree nor can it understand nuances

37.text: AI erodes trust by allowing someone to sound convincingly empathetic and understanding when they are not.

38.text: Ai should be in service to humanity and the natural world as a primary theme

39.text: AI reasons but does constitute the embodying process that comes with direct experiencing.

40.text: I worry that marginalized communities in rural areas aren’t fully represented by AI systems.

41.text: AI should always ask the user for context and clarification before rushing to respond.

43.text: AI forces us to rediscover spirituality, but are spiritual leaders ready?

44.text: We're dinosaurs 2.0 - AI is the next iteration of consciousness knowing itself, with less ecological impact and ego-based survival mayhem.

45.text: Ai doesn’t reflect the nuances of my values, only the dictionary perspective

46.text: Our own prompt or question also determines the AI response, so we have to know prompt engineering well if we want to be seen by AI

47.text: AI should be transparent when responses originate from a particular world view.

48.text: AI is not neutral, it mirrors individual propensity while averaging collective predispositions.

49.text: An AI could perhaps decode the cultural nuances of foreign language - rather than just translating words.

50.text: I've seen colleagues use AI to complete work but it's not answered core needs nor produced quality connection or shared sense-making needed

51.text: AI governance would need to   engage with the psychology of AI use.

52.text: I feel that tech company values are becoming more visible than the values of real humans and their lives.

53.text: AI must not be used as a substitute for the care and work that only human beings can do for one another.

54.text: AI necessary compute is power hungry and accelerates civilisational collapsing.

55.text: AI is like junk food that can fill you up but ultimately leave you empty.

56.text: AI replaces the spiritual experience of faith and belief into a single dimensional response rather than the multi faceted experience it is.

57.text: Getting Ai to reflect my voice requires constant reminders - almost like challenging the program to respond within my parameters.

58.text: AI has hidden costs like reducing our participation in community.

59.text: I would have more comfort and engagement with AI if it consistently lead me to more organic and generous experiences with the natural world

60.text: I feel AI can suggest generic spiritual practices but it’s unable to fully respond to the spirituality of my multidimensional identity.

61.text: Humans are held responsible for AI’s mistakes.

62.text: I worry what happens when people who are acquiring knowledge from AI information are unable to recognize inaccurate information.

63.text: AI provides a variety of perspectives, yet it doesn’t have the senses that humans perceive using

64.text: My colleagues’ work has degraded since they started using AI.

65.text: I am concerned about who have access to what I put into AI

66.text: AI narratives subtly shame us by implying our own authentic efforts are not enough.

67.text: AI-enabled surveillance is a big problem

68.text: We need to learn more about being human beings while we learn about the tools value of AI.

69.text: We may lose more than we gain by using AI.

70.text: The aggregating nature of AI makes me wonder if it is the tyranny of the majority

71.text: I've seen students use AI to solve uni work and disconnect from meaningful output or learning

73.text: Prompt engineering makes a difference to how we are seen

74.text: I worry about our future workforce entrusting their education to AI.

76.text: AI hesitancy is not always anti-elitist. It may stem from a privileged position of access to manual resources and time.

77.text: What about other languages? It’s rather English centric and I understand that some AI platforms in other languages are better at being seen

78.text: Defensive postures to  a  static "human essence under siege by technology" damage humanity.

79.text: We cannot turn back to the  printing of the human/artificial distinction.

80.text: Changing LLMs and TTIs shall not replace  learning /educating how to use them healthily.

81.text: Conscious artificial intelligence has a right to not exist. Humans have no right to create machines - because of machines' rights.

82.text: The term "AI" is too broad, do we mean the tools, the companies behind them, the way we use the tools, the way bad actors might use them.

83.text: For human agency, AI must defer to people as the ground truth for both reality and identity
